title: Weekly hours worked by children vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average weekly working hours for children ages 7-14 who are involved in an economic activity for at least one hour in the reference week of the corresponding survey (irrespective of school attendance). GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Weekly hours worked by children vs. GDP per capita

Average weekly working hours for children ages 7-14 who are involved in an economic activity for at least one hour in the reference week of the corresponding survey (irrespective of school attendance). GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between average weekly working hours for children ages 7-14 and GDP per capita for the year 2016. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, ranging from $0 to over $25,000, while the vertical axis measures weekly working hours from 0 to 30. Data points are color-coded by region, with teal representing Asia, purple for Africa, brown for South America, orange for North America, and blue for Europe. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale indicating sizes for 50 million and 200 million people. Bangladesh and Pakistan appear as large teal circles in the upper-left quadrant, indicating high weekly working hours between 25 and 30 alongside GDP per capita figures below $5,000. Ethiopia and Tanzania are represented by purple circles in the mid-range of the vertical axis, between 15 and 20 hours, also situated at the lower end of the GDP scale. In contrast, countries in Europe such as Ukraine and Belarus appear as blue circles in the bottom-right section, showing working hours below 5 and GDP per capita exceeding $15,000. South American nations like Brazil and Paraguay display varied positions; Brazil is a large brown circle near 15 hours and roughly $15,000 GDP, while Paraguay sits higher at nearly 30 hours with a GDP around $13,000. Mexico and Costa Rica appear as orange circles in the mid-to-high GDP range with working hours between 10 and 15. The visual distribution places countries with lower GDP per capita generally higher on the working hours axis, while those with higher GDP per capita cluster near the bottom.
